question_text: Propose hypothetical reaction coordinate diagrams for: (a) An endergonic, two-step reaction with the first step being the rate-determining step. (b) A reaction with three transition states and ΔG > 0. (c) A three-step reaction where K > 1, and the third step is faster than the first step, which is faster than the second step. (d) A one-step reaction where the conversion to reactants is faster than conversion to products. Is ΔG positive or negative?
canonical_answer: (a) Energy is plotted on the vertical axis, reaction progress on the horizontal axis. The graph starts with a plateau (reactant), passes over a first barrier, and falls into a valley (intermediate), which is higher in energy than the starting plateau. Then, a transition occurs over a second barrier to a final plateau (product). The product plateau is higher in energy than the reactant plateau, but lower than the intermediate valley. The energy of the first barrier is higher than the energy of the second barrier.; (b) Energy is plotted on the vertical axis, reaction progress on the horizontal axis. The graph starts with a plateau (reactant), passes over a first barrier, and falls into a valley (intermediate), which is higher in energy than the starting plateau. Then it crosses a second barrier, which is the highest peak, and falls into a second valley (intermediate), which is higher in energy than the first intermediate valley. Finally, it passes over a third barrier, which is higher in energy than first barrier, but lower in energy than second barrier, and drops to a final plateau (product), which is higher in energy than the starting reactant plateau, but lower than the first intermediate valley.; (c) Energy is plotted on the vertical axis, reaction progress on the horizontal axis. The graph starts with a plateau (reactant), passes over a first barrier, and falls into a valley (intermediate). Energy of first intermediate is higher, than the reactant energy. Then it rises to a second barrier, which is the highest point on the graph, and falls into a second valley, which is lower in energy than first valley, but higher in energy than the reactant plateau. Then, a transition occurs over a third barrier, which is the lowest of the three, to a final plateau (product). The product plateau is lower in energy than the starting reactant plateau. The third barrier is lower in energy than first intermediate.; (d) Energy is plotted on the vertical axis, reaction progress on the horizontal axis. The graph starts with a plateau (reactant), rises steeply over a single barrier, and drops slightly to a final plateau (product). The product plateau is higher in energy than the starting reactant plateau. ΔG is positive

question_text: Provide the mechanisms and products for the following reactions. Provide distinct structures for all intermediates. First reaction is C=CCCCCN + Br2, second reaction is C=C(C)C(C)c1ccccc1 + HBr.
canonical_answer: The first reaction presents the intramolecular cyclization of an amino-alkene in the presence of bromine. The mechanism initiates with the formation of a bromonium ion, where a curved arrow originates from the alkene double bond and points to a bromine atom in the diatomic bromine molecule, while a second arrow indicates the breakage of the Br-Br bond. This results in a three-membered cyclic bromonium intermediate. Next, the nitrogen atom of the amine group acts as an internal nucleophile; an arrow goes from the nitrogen lone pair to a carbon atom of the bromonium ring to create a six-membered ring, while another arrow points from the C-Br bond to the bromine atom to open the three-membered ring. This yields a positively charged ammonium intermediate. In the final step, a bromide ion deprotonates the nitrogen, indicated by an arrow moving from the bromide ion to the hydrogen atom attached to the nitrogen, followed by an arrow going from the N-H bond to the nitrogen atom to neutralize the charge, resulting in the final piperidine product. The second reaction is the addition of HBr to an alkene involving a carbocation rearrangement. The process begins with the protonation of the alkene, where an arrow originates from the double bond and points to the hydrogen atom of the HBr molecule, and another arrow points from the H-Br bond to the bromine atom to release a bromide ion. This generates an initial secondary carbocation. A rearrangement then occurs via a 1,2-hydride shift, indicated by a small arrow starting at the adjacent benzylic C-H bond and pointing to the positively charged carbon. This shift creates a more stable benzylic carbocation, which is stabilized by resonance. Finally, the reaction concludes with the nucleophilic attack of the bromide ion, where an arrow extends from the bromide lone pair to the positively charged benzylic carbon, forming the final rearranged alkyl bromide product.

question_text: Provide a detailed mechanism for the following transformation. Propose all the reagents used. Ignore stereochemistry. Reactant is CCCC1=CCCC1. Product is CCCC1CCCC1Br
canonical_answer: Reagents are HBr and ROOR.  The mechanism is divided into Initiation and propagation sections. In the initiation a peroxide molecule ROOR undergoes thermal homolytic cleavage to yield two alkoxy radicals. In the second step, an alkoxy radical reacts with hydrogen bromide where a single-headed curved arrow extends from the oxygen radical electron and meets a corresponding arrow from the HBr bond to form an alcohol, while a third single-headed arrow points from the H-Br bond to the bromine atom to generate a bromine radical. In the propagation phase, the bromine radical adds to 1-propylcyclopentene where a single-headed arrow originates from the double bond and meets a single-headed arrow from the bromine radical to form a CBr bond at the secondary carbon, while another single-headed arrow moves from the double bond to the tertiary carbon creating a tertiary radical intermediate. In the second step, this intermediate abstracts a hydrogen from HBr shown by a single-headed arrow from the carbon radical electron meeting a single-headed arrow from the HBr bond to form a CH bond, and a final single-headed arrow points from the HBr bond to the bromine atom to yield the final alkyl bromide product and regenerate a bromine radical.

question_text: Triphenylphosphine can be used in a two-step process to convert E alkenes to Z alkenes or vice-versa. In this example, the E starting material is first converted to the epoxide, which is then treated with Ph3P. Propose a likely mechanism, using stereochemically correct structures, for the phosphine-mediated part of the process. In the {box_1}, provide the structure of a reagent for converting the alkene to the epoxide.
canonical_answer: {box_1}:= O=C(OO)c1cccc(Cl)c1. (E)-1-phenylprop-1-ene undergoes epoxidation with meta-chloroperoxybenzoic acid to yield trans-2-methyl-3-phenyloxirane. Triphenylphosphine nucleophilically attacks the epoxide carbon, triggering C-O bond cleavage and generating a zwitterionic betaine intermediate containing a phosphonium cation and an alkoxide anion. Following rotation around the central carbon-carbon bond, the alkoxide attacks the phosphorus center to form a four-membered oxaphosphetane ring. A subsequent concerted syn-cycloreversion eliminates triphenylphosphine oxide and produces (Z)-1-phenylprop-1-ene.

question_text: The antidepressant drug tranylcypromine is a primary amine with the amino group on a cyclopropane ring. Propose how you would convert an enantiomer of 2-phenylcyclopropanecarboxylic acid (which is made from trans-cinnamic acid) to tranylcypromine. (Hint: If someone is asking you to rearrange your atoms, it is best to be courteous.). What reaction would you use to effect the first transformation written below? Trans-cinnamic acid is O=C(O)/C=C/c1ccccc1, 2-phenylcyclopropanecarboxylic acid is [H][C@]1(C(=O)O)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2, tranylcypromine is [H][C@]1(N)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2.

[H][C@]1(C(=O)O)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2 + SOCl2 = [H][C@]1(C(=O)Cl)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2, [H][C@]1(C(=O)Cl)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2 + NaN3 = [H][C@]1(C(=O)N=N#N)C[C@]1([H])c2ccccc2, [H][C@]1(C(=O)N=N#N)C[C@]1([H])c2ccccc2 + heat, then H2O = [H][C@]1(N)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2; [H][C@]1(C(=O)O)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2 + SOCl2 = [H][C@]1(C(=O)Cl)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2,  [H][C@]1(C(=O)Cl)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2 + NH3, then Br2 + NaOH + H2O = [H][C@]1(N)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2.

question_text: Richard Willstatter (1915 Chemistry Nobel Laureate) treated diamine A with excess methyl iodide, and then with excess Ag2O and heat, whereupon a hydrocarbon B, C8H8, distilled from the reaction mixture. Compound B rapidly reacted with excess Br2 under mild conditions. Treatment of compound C in the same way gave a hydrocarbon D, C6H6, which did not react with excess Br2. Identify compounds B, D, and E and in a sentence or two explain the different reactivity of B and D towards Br2 (Willstatter concluded from these experiments that compound D could not be an alkene). What is the name of the reaction that converts A into B and C into D? compound A CN(C)C1C/C=C\C(N(C)C)C/C=C\1 + 1) MeI (excess), 2) Ag2O, Δ = compound B {box_1}. compound B + Br2 (excess) = BrC1C(Br)C(Br)C(Br)C(Br)C(Br)C(Br)C1Br. compound C CN(C)C1C=CC(N(C)C)CC1 + 1) MeI (excess), 2) Ag2O, Δ = compound D {box_2}. compound D + Br2 (excess) = no reaction.
canonical_answer: {box_1}=C1=C\C=C/C=C\C=C/1; {box_2}=c1ccccc1; Compound D (benzene) is very stable because it is aromatic and therefore does not react with Br2. Compound B is not aromatic and consequently is less stable and more prone to reacting with Br2. The reaction in question is Hofmann reaction

[H][C@](O)(C=O)[C@@]([H])(O)[C@]([H])(O)[C@]([H])(O)CO + NH2OH = {box_1} (compound A), {box_1} + Ac2O (excess) = {box_2} (compound B), {box_2} + NaOAc = [H][C@@](C#N)(OC(C)=O)[C@@]([H])(OC(C)=O)[C@]([H])(OC(C)=O)[C@@]([H])(COC(C)=O)OC(C)=O (compound C), [H][C@@](C#N)(OC(C)=O)[C@@]([H])(OC(C)=O)[C@]([H])(OC(C)=O)[C@@]([H])(COC(C)=O)OC(C)=O + NaOCH3 + CH3OH = [H][C@@](O)(CO)[C@@]([H])(O)[C@]([H])(O)C(C)=O (arabinose).
canonical_answer: {box_1}:= [H][C@](O)(/C=N\O)[C@@]([H])(O)[C@]([H])(O)[C@]([H])(O)CO, {box_2}:= [H][C@@](/C=N\OC(C)=O)(OC(C)=O)[C@@]([H])(OC(C)=O)[C@]([H])(OC(C)=O)[C@@]([H])(COC(C)=O)OC(C)=O. Mechanism for conversion of B to C: the transformation of the peracetylated oxime into the nitrile is initiated by the acetate anion, which acts as a base to abstract the proton from the imine carbon. The electron pair from the broken carbon-hydrogen bond moves to establish a carbon-nitrogen triple bond, facilitating the simultaneous heterolytic cleavage of the nitrogen-oxygen bond and the expulsion of the acetoxy leaving group. Mechanism for conversion of C to arabinose: the degradation process begins with the nucleophilic attack of a methoxide anion on the carbonyl carbon of the C2-acetoxy group. This addition forms a tetrahedral intermediate which collapses to expel methyl acetate, generating a C2-alkoxide anion. The negative charge on the oxygen atom then drives the formation of a new carbonyl double bond, triggering the cleavage of the C1–C2 sigma bond and the expulsion of the cyanide anion to yield the chain-shortened aldehyde.

question_text:
Compound A has the molecular formula C6H10O3 and shows a strong IR absorption peak near 1710 cm-1. When treated with I2 in aqueous sodium hydroxide, A gives a yellow precipitate. When A is treated with Tollen's reagent (Ag+),
no reaction occurs. However, if A is treated first with water containing a drop of sulfuric acid a new compound is isolated and is found to have a 1H NMR spectrum which includes a downfield triplet at 9.5 ppm. Compound A shows the following 1H NMR spectrum: singlet (2.13 ppm, 3H), doublet (2.75 ppm, 2H), multiplet (3.95 ppm, 2H), multiplet (4.05 ppm, 2H), triplet (5.12 ppm, 1H). 13C signals for A: 205.1, 108.4, 64.4 (2C), 48.2, 30.1 ppm. Deduce the structure of A, and write annotated reactions to explain the outcome of each chemical test. Calculate the degree of unsaturation for A.
canonical_answer: According to spectral data and chemical properties, the structure of A is CC(CC1OCCO1)=O. Iodoform test is positive: CC(CC1OCCO1)=O + I2, NaOH = O=C(O[Na])CC1OCCO1 + CHI3 (yellow precipitate). Tollen's test is negative, because the is no aldehyde present for oxidation. The third reaction is acetal cleavage: CC(CC1OCCO1)=O + H+, H2O = CC(CC=O)=O. The degree of unsaturation equals 2.

Predict the principal products expected (if any) for the following reaction sequences. Provide accurate depictions of stereochemical outcomes. O=C(OC(C)(C)C)N(CC=C)CC=C + 1) (PCy3)2Cl2Ru=CHPh (cat), CH2Cl2, 2) mCPBA, CH2Cl2, 3) Ph2Cu(CN)Li2, THF, – 20 °C, 4) TFA, CH2Cl2 = {box_5)

Crellinose is a reducing disaccharide which undergoes mutarotation. Hydrolysis by either aqueous acid or α-arabinosidases give L-arabinose and D-glucose. Oxidation of crellinose with bromine water followed by acid hydrolysis gives L-arabinose and D-gluconic acid. Methylation of the bromine-water oxidation product, followed by acid hydrolysis gives 2,3,4-tri-O-methyl-L-arabinose and 2,3,4,5-tetra-O-methyl-D-gluconic acid. Methylation and acid hydrolysis of rellinose gives 2,3,4-tri-O-methyl-L-arabinose and 2,3,4-tri-O-methyl-D-glucose. What is the structure of crellinose? Explain the experimental data.
canonical_answer: The observation that crellinose is a reducing sugar and undergoes mutarotation implies that there is a hemiacetal carbon present. Since the glycosidic bond is cleaved by α-arabinosidase, the linkage must be of α configuration. Oxidation of crellinose produces D-gluconic acid, which indicates that the hemiacetal carbon is located on the glucose component of the disaccharide. Since acid hydrolysis of the methylated oxidation product yields 2,3,4,5-tetra-O-methyl-D-gluconic acid, the glycosidic linkage must occur between the anomeric carbon of the arabinose component and the sixth carbon of the glucose component (since there is no methylation observed on the C6 alcohol). The glucose portion of the methylation and acid hydrolysis product of crellinose is 2,3,4-tri-O-methyl-D-glucose because the alcohol on C5 is tied up in the ring bonded to C1. Therefore, the structure of crellinose consists of a pyranose L-arabinose linked by α-1,6-linkage to a pyranose D-glucose, O[C@@H]1[C@H](O)[C@@H](O)[C@H](OC[C@@H]2[C@@H](O)[C@H](O)[C@@H](O)[C@@H](O)O2)OC1.

question_text:
The complete structure of JZ, a polypeptide with antibiotic properties, has been worked out as follows:
I) Analysis of the hydrolysis products gave equimolar amounts of Leu, Orn, Phe, Pro, Val. (Ornithine, Orn, is a rare amino
acid whose formula is shown.) N[C@@H](CCCN)C(O)=O
II) Measurement of the molecular weight gave an approximate value of 1250.
III) Sanger N-terminal residue analysis with 1-fluoro-2,4-dinitrobenzene gave only DNB-NHCH2CH2CH2CH(NH3+)CO2–.
IV) Partial hydrolysis of JZ gave the following di- and tri-peptides: Leu-Phe, Orn-Leu, Phe-Pro, Val-Orn, Phe-Pro-Val, Pro-Val-
Orn, Val-Orn-Leu.

Explain the results of the Sanger experiment (statement III) and determine the structure of JZ. Write the structure using three-
letter amino acid representation
canonical_answer: The results of the Sanger experiment indicate that no N terminus is present in the polypeptide since the FDNB did not react with any free alpha-amino groups other than the one on the Orn side chain. The structure of JZ: cyclo-(Orn-Lue-Phe-Pro-Val-Orn-Leu-Phe-Pro-Val).

C1=CC=CC=C1 + AcCl, FeCl3 = CC(C1=CC=CC=C1)=O; CC(C1=CC=CC=C1)=O + 1) LDA, 2) O=CC1=CC=C([N+]([O-])=O)C=C1, then heat = O=C(/C=C/C1=CC=C([N+]([O-])=O)C=C1)C2=CC=CC=C2.

question_text: Propose a synthesis of the following molecule from succinic acid. You have at your disposal any reagents containing four carbons or fewer. (Hint: A good synthetic route takes advantage of the symmetry present in the target molecule.) O=C(CC1(C)C(OCC=C)=O)C(C(OCC=C)=O)(C)CC1=O
canonical_answer: OC(CCC(O)=O)=O + C=CCO (2 equivalents) + TsOH = O=C(CCC(OCC=C)=O)OCC=C; O=C(CCC(OCC=C)=O)OCC=C + 1) NaH (2 equivalents), C=CCO, 2) HCl, H2O = OC1=C(C(OCC=C)=O)CC(O)=C(C(OCC=C)=O)C1; OC1=C(C(OCC=C)=O)CC(O)=C(C(OCC=C)=O)C1 + MeI (2 equivalents), K2CO3 (2 equivalents) = O=C(CC1(C)C(OCC=C)=O)C(C(OCC=C)=O)(C)CC1=O.
